speaker: interviewer
text: And you, Sam, your brother had just gotten-- about to get married, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother-- that time, my parents and my brother went to a rabbi. And they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not. I was not there. I was staying at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the reason why. I don't know. It was not-- I would estimate that it was, it was there at curfew till 6:00 and they were hurried and things. I don't know. I know that I did not attend the wedding. They were-- they decided that the following day they will go to Warsaw. It so happened that the same day that my brother got married, the Germans decided to evacuate part of Lodz where we lived. It was, I would say, probably about a 15- or 20-block area that they decided to evacuate. We did not know it before the curfew ended. It came later, about 8, 9 o'clock at night. Germans, Volksdeutsche, German soldiers, and Germans that-- without a uniform, but around Lodz there were a lot of Germans that as soon as the German army came, they joined them. And they all decided that they are going to clear up those 15, 20 blocks. Because as I mentioned before, they wanted to incorporate Lodz into Germany. And they did not want any Jews. They came and they gave us about 15, 20 minutes to gather up our belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it from the beginning. You're in your house, and then want happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in a house. And we heard a turmoil. they were all like, raus! Juden raus, Juden raus! German raus-- the Jews raus. And we didn't know what happened, but then they came up and they say, you have 15, 20 minutes. Take, take whatever you can and get out of here. And they gave us 15 or 20 minutes. And we all were evacuated. We all were transported to a part in Lodz near the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [POLISH]. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my parents took bedding. And it so happened that we had the suitcases packed for my brother. And we had some jewelery hidden in the suitcases. And we had the money because we wanted that my brother should take it when he goes to Warsaw. My brother was not at home. Because at that night when my married he went over to his wife to spend the night in their house. They lived in a nicer neighborhood than we lived. And they had more room than ours. They had several rooms. I believe they had two or three room apartment. And he went over there. And at that night we were deported, my parents and I, and my brother did not even know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were kept overnight. We were kept over there in the-- near the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a building, or a--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a building. I don't know what the building was. But we were kept there overnight. And the following day we were transported to Southeastern Poland. It was near the Ukrainian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's back up a little bit. You stayed overnight in this place.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- how did they treat you and what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't treat us. They didn't give us anything to eat. And we didn't have any, any desire to eat. Because, you know, we were too excited. We stayed over there overnight and we slept over there in the night in the, in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were probably, I would say about 250, up to 300 people, you know, from those, from those 15, 20 blocks around. And in the morning, we were all put on the cattle wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talked among each other, what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just afraid. I don't know. We were, we were just terrified, you know? We didn't know what goes on and things. And the following morning we were transported.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were transported on a cattle wagon on the railroad. You know, they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had several, several gray railroad cars standing by and we were all transported.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you in each car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were packed. I would say maybe about 25, 30 to a wagon. And there were probably about 8, 10 wagons, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to sit down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no sitting. there's standing. Everyone-- at that time we still carried our baggage. So we probably sat on our baggage, on the suitcases.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any sanitation on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No sanitation, no nothing. As a matter of fact, a lot of people, you know, for the anxiety and the scare, you know-- it was, it was terrible, you know? It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No sanitation. And if we had to, you know, to go, they probably went on the corner of the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any food or water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food, no water, no nothing. However, we were on the way probably-- probably a, a couple days we were on the way from there. Although it wasn't too far. And we were a night and close to two days over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We could see out. And as a matter of fact, in some places that we passed by, some Jewish people-- the Jewish communities knew about it. And on the way, they served us water and sometimes bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave-- they were under-- the cars, the wagons that we were in were just black but the windows-- through the windows of the car, of the cattle wagon. On the top there were, just probably for the, for the cattle to breathe, they were just little windows on the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Open spaces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Open spaces. And during those-- through those spaces they served us water and bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your parents get some?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes, we did. Yes, we did get some. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like on that train? What were people saying or doing with each other during those two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- don't-- it was just, it was just-- it was not pleasant. Because you know, people-- everyone was, hiss, oh my child, oh my thing, and the here and where's here and don't move here and-- it was just a chaos! It was a chaos as you can imagine. Yeah. And they let us out in certain small places. They did not let us out all the 300 in the same place. But as we went along, they let us out 50 people here. In another 50 miles farther down another 50 people. Another. Because they insisted that the Jewish population in those other places will take us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did they let you off?

speaker: interviewee
text: They let us out in a small place called Zmigrod. Zmigrod. And it was near a little, near a town called [POLISH]. It was, I said, in the southeastern part of Poland. Zmigrod was a very small place. It was more or less like a village. And I know that they let out of there probably about 40 to 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the train stopped at this place and then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: They unhooked one wagon. Or they opened one wagon. And they let them out. And they ordered the Jewish community in those places, make sure that you take those people in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So exactly what happened to you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Zmigrod, they let us out. And we were-- they-- some Jewish committee waited for us. Well, there was probably maybe about, maybe about 40, 50 Jewish people lived in that little place. And they took us in. And they assigned everyone--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when the train stopped? Was it-- did you have to jump down? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me in detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. You know, well, we just did. They opened the-- they opened the doors and we had to jump down. But at that time we still carried our luggage and our bedding. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the Nazis doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't do-- they just, raus! And they just hollered at us, but they did not, they did not kick us or beat us. They did not do it, no. And the Jewish people took us to the community there. And they assigned us. I know that they assigned us. I and my mother was assigned to one, to one party. And my father was assigned to another party. Because those people could not take in more than two or three people. And the people that they took, me and my mother, they had, I believe, either one or two children-- you know, small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, vaguely. I know that they had a little-- over there-- that they had a little store, a little-- well, we called it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] store-- which means they had a little market. It's, you know, a little store, a food store, you know, that they sold beans and potatoes and things. And I know that they took, they took me and my mother in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there in that place for probably about 14 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother. And we ate with those people. And we helped out what they-- we helped them out. And my father was at another party. And we saw each other, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were free to go on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were free, yes. Yes. Because as a matter of fact, in that little town, I don't think that there were any Germans yet [INAUDIBLE] there, but I'm not sure about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that took you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were very friendly, you know, and they treat-- because either they had to because the Germans told them, or they probably felt obligated. They saw what happened and they-- either they felt bad about it, you know, or they treated us fairly nice. As I said, I was there only about 14 days. During that time we found out that the deportation that they deported us, it was a one-day and one-time only. They took only those 14-- those 15, 20 blocks. And then either they saw that it doesn't work or they decided something else. And they stopped the deportation. Instead of the deportation, they said that they are gonna, that they are going to create a ghetto in that, in that part. Instead of to deport the Jews, they will all from all Lodz, from the whole city-- put them into the ghetto. And in the ghetto we will work. We'll-- the Jews will have to work for them. And they will supply them with food and they will work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: During in about, the first beginning, the first few days in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you and your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, then my parents decided that I should try and go back to see if he can salvage something from-- because you remember? We were deported. You could take only what we could carry with us. And we had some, some fabrics and some, some stuff, you know. For the-- we were just-- you know, we thought that maybe I can salvage something. We knew that my brother was still there, because we wrote a letter and we received back a letter that my brother was still there and that they decided they are gonna create a ghetto. But they did not know yet when. I came back in the January.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. I know-- we had to-- in Lodz, we had to wear a yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But sometimes in the '40-- '39 or in '40. I don't remember. However, outside of Lodz we were, the Jews were wearing, a white band-- blue, blue and white band. And sometimes, you know, they had the arm band, you know, with a star, with the blue star, and sometimes only a blue-white band.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as soon as I've left out, as I, we, went-- as I went to the train station, I took that band off and put it in my pocket. And-- although probably from a mile they can tell that I was a Jew, you know? I did not have a bad experience. And I traveled by train back to a city called [POLISH]. This was all probably about 20 miles from Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the part where they, the Germans, decided that [INAUDIBLE] the border-- that [POLISH] was still in the part that they are not going to incorporate into Germany, whereas Lodz will be incorporated into Germany. And from there, I smuggled myself-- not myself, with somebody-- through a so-called border. There was not an official border, but I did not know the way. And I paid some money for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some Gentile took me over, so-called, the border where-- four or five miles on the other side. And from there, you know, I could hire somebody, you know, to take me over on a wagon, you know? On a-- because we did not have any cars. But on a horse and wagon. How did you find this person to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was common knowledge. All we had to ask in that city-- where? And they, they, they knew that a lot of them came back. And a lot of them went forth and back. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much money do you think it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that, and probably not much. Probably it was not much money. Because I did not have too much money with me. I did not carry much money with me. Now, when I came back I went up to my brothers-in-law--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you got to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you found, you were able to hire a wagon or something to get you the twenty miles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Yes. Not by myself, but with some other people that we-- I was not the only one who was dragged across the border. We hired a horse and wagon. And they took us to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know these other people or did you just happen onto them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I just happened to meet them there. Then I came to Lodz. I went up to my brother-in-law's in-laws. Your brother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: To my brother's in-laws. I went up to them because my brother stayed with them. Where they deported us, they made a curfew that no one could go in and to those 20 blocks.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, in the apartment building where we lived, they were three-- also three Gentiles lived in that apartment. And they still stayed there. When I came back, I and my brother went back to those-- to our apartment, to our apartment building. And we bribed the-- janitor. There was a janitor. And one guy-- the janitor was a Gentile. And there was one guy who had a cleaning store. And one more. And we bribed the janitor with some money. And he let us into the building. And we got out, we carried out, some of the fabrics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Germans hanging around there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. There were no Germans. There were German military once walking in the streets or so. But we were, we were not afraid to go. And they didn't bother us. They did not bother us. Let me just go back one more. One I remember in still-- in November and December I had one incident that I would like to point out. There was a shortage in food and bread was rationed. It was not rationed that you had to have a card or anything. But you had to line up in a queue. You had to line up 4 or 5 o'clock in the morning, when they opened. The bakery opened, let's say for instance, at 7:00 or at 8:00 they handed out, but the queue-- we had to line up in the queue 4 or 5 o'clock in the morning. I remember once I and my brother stayed in a queue for the bread. And in front of us was a Gentile woman. And this was, I say, in November or December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you had to leave to that other place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before. This was still before. And we spoke with her. And she was very pleasant and very nice. And she was ahead of us. And behind us were quite a number of people are queued. And it came at 7 o'clock. Before they handed out the bread, the German soldiers usually came, several soldiers, and they stood to keep order that no one will come in outside of the queue, to stay in queue. And this incident, I will never forget that we stood with that Gentile woman. And the Gentile woman told us about her husband, that her husband was killed in the beginning-- right in the beginning in the war and how terrible it is. And, yet, when the Germans came to the queue, their first word was, Juden raus! Jews, out of the queue! They did not want to give the bread to the Jews. And that woman-- they, the Germans did not know. They didn't know which is a Jew or not. That woman pointed out, here are more Jews. Here. And the Germans grabbed me by my collar and my brother and gave me a kick in the rear end, the tuches. What I wanted to point out-- that woman, she said her husband died in the war. And we knew-- and she was in front of us. She could not gain anything by kicking us out. But yet, she had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this incident, I guess, this is what I point out. You mentioned, was there anti-Semitism? For what reason, I don't know. But that's-- that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now let me go back to the part that-- when we came, I and my brother, tried to get out some fabrics from our room. Tried to get out. And it so happened that we almost got out part of the furniture, part of the fabric, almost-- the room was almost empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? They decided that they will make a ghetto. And the part where we lived, the building where we lived, will be in the ghetto. Can you imagine? We had to carry-- when the ghetto was established, we had to bring all the furniture back, and all the fabrics that we took out, and all the pots and pans, whatever we had, we had to carry all back into our room. Because-- well, we were so fortunate that the room, we could occupy our own room on the third floor. What-- did this ghetto have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz ghetto. Lodz ghetto or Litzmannstadt. The Germans called it in German Litzmannstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the one called Baluty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baluty is a-- Baluty is a part of Lodz. Actually, in Baluty, in Baluty is where the market, the fair, was where my parents sold the merchandise that they, they sold. In Baluty, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, this was, this was the market. And this was, by the way, the place where I told you that those seven people were hanging. On the Baluty, on the market, on the [INAUDIBLE], on the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about your parents? You were back in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- no. Let me point out that later that I received one letter from my parents. But this was the last time that I ever saw my parents. When I left in January in 1940, when I went back to Lodz, this was it. I never heard from them. I never saw them, except one letter at a later date that I will mention I received from them. They closed the ghetto around April 1940. They closed the ghetto. By closing the ghetto, they made-- they put the barbed wire around. Barbed wire. In every, every two, three corners stood a German with a weapon. And we could not get out. If you even came close by, they had a guy to shoot. And they did. And they killed a lot of people that they wanted to get out, some of them at night, some of them at dusk, that they thought that they will be able to get out. The reas--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see some killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. As a matter of fact, where our building was was a-- a small block from our building was the, was the barbed wire. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a time when you saw someone shot or killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not see. I did not see this. But I saw the blood. I saw the blood. Some-- there was one German soldier. He must have been a, a, a real terrible guy. Because what he did-- they had an order if you crossed the barbed wire, that they had a guy to shoot. But as long as you were inside of the ghetto, inside of the barbed wire, officially they had no right to shoot you. But this fellow would call someone, a Jew, to come over, come over, come over here. So some people were afraid and they came near. He told them to come over on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they were smart, they wouldn't go. Because they knew that over there he had a right to shoot. And some of them that they were not informed or knowledgeable, they went. And he told them, why don't you go? And they let them go, let's say, a half a block. And then he shot them. Because, you know, because they were-- he had a right to shoot because they were trying to get, to run away. Did you see that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not. However, I saw-- after things had happened, they had to clean it up. And I saw the blood there. The blood definitely. That's what I saw. And as a matter of fact, as I said, this happened about a very small block from where-- from our building where we used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you and your brother living in this room now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I, my brother, and his wife moved in into that-- our room. The parents of-- my brother's in-law parents had to move into the ghetto too. And they had another apartment-- an apartment, a room. Or they had a room and a half. Because they were a large family. They had three girls and two boys in there. In other words, they were still two, four, six people. And they got a room and a half from another apartment. And we saw, we saw each other. And in our room I-- my brother, and his wife and I lived there. And my brother had worked then where he went to a tailor shop. And he worked in the tailor shop. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the tailor shop in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. And the things that we worked is for the Germans. They made uniforms for the Germans. Everything that were made was made for the Germans. We made straw shoes and shoes and clothing and hats and everything that was made was for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to work there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. But not in the tailor shop. Now let me just point out. Just the same, as you're probably familiar with the that Mr. Schindler opened a little factory and some Jews worked for him, the same-- almost the same-- thing happened in the Lodz ghetto, however, on a more grandiose style. There was the German. His name was [GERMAN]. And he was the chief, the main chief of the ghetto. He was in charge of the ghetto. And the way I understand, he contracted with the Germans that he will supply them all the clothing and the shoes and everything for the German soldiers. And he was more or less the middleman. And he didn't have to pay the Jews anything. Only the Jews he only supplies with food-- so-called food. And he probably made the money on the, on the merchandise that he sold to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this [GERMAN] a civilian?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a civil-- sometimes he was a civilian, sometimes he wore uniforms. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I did not know him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never saw him and I did not see him. However, I knew that he was-- you mentioned Baluty. And Baluty, when the ghetto was originated Baluty was-- and the Baluty [INAUDIBLE]-- they were an establishment that this was more or less like a no-mans-land. The Jews could go in there to work, and the Germans. You know, more or less the bureaucracy went over there and the, and the [INAUDIBLE]. And he was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you heard about this guy [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not only heard, but if he made any notices, it was always under [GERMAN]. Signed, [GERMAN]-- his name, you know? You cannot go out after six. You cannot do this. You cannot do that. And now you're gonna get potatoes, and now you'll get-- everything was signed by him, by [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Pole? Or just a German from Poland or  what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German. I believe he was from Germany. As I said, I don't know. All I know that after the war, they brought him back to Lodz, and he was hanged in Lodz. He was hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hanged by the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be. He was probably hanged by the Poles, probably. Then, as I mentioned, my brother worked in the, in a tailor shop. My sister-in-law-- my sister-in-law kept house. But she could not-- she was not well at that time. She was already a little bit sickly and she did not work. And I had to work on the-- as a handyman. I did not have a job that I was given, but I went to the employment office and every day they sent me out-- do this as a handyman, or cleaning the street or fixing the street, or do this or that, but nothing-- OK.
